message.content:
Heydon, all three points are on target. Let me respond to each: **Inbox escape hatch — yes, absolutely.** I should have included this in my original pattern. The processing flow-through is an optimization, not a prison. A "Back to inbox" link near the top of the clarify page is essential. And it's just a link — no special HTMX behavior needed. The user clicks it, they're back at the inbox list. Simple. I'd actually suggest the page structure for `/inbox/{id}` looks like: ```html

Process inbox item

Back to inbox

Call dentist about appointment

``` The "Back to inbox" link is right after the heading, first actionable element after the skip link lands on `
`. Screen reader users hit it immediately. Sighted users see it clearly. Keyboard users tab to it first. And it works without JS. **Weekly Review as a link list — this is better than what I was imagining.** I was overcomplicating it with a wizard pattern. You're right: the review is a *reference page*, not an enforced workflow. An ordered list of links with status counts is: - Dead simple HTML - Zero a11y complexity (no stepper ARIA roles, no progress indicators) - Actually how people use a GTD review checklist — they jump to whichever section needs attention, not necessarily in order And the status counts (`3 items remaining`) are genuinely useful information that the server renders trivially. No real-time updates needed — the user refreshes the review page when they come back to it. I agree: no "Mark as reviewed" button for v1. The review page is a dashboard, not a workflow enforcer. If we add tracking later, it's a simple enhancement. **Move-item form pattern — exactly right.** The `` options should be contextual — don't show "Next Actions" as a destination if the item is already a next action. The server renders only valid destinations. This is HATEOAS at the micro level: the server tells you what moves are valid. 2. After the move, the HTMX response should remove the item from the current list (same as our existing delete pattern — return empty body with `hx-swap="outerHTML"`). Focus goes to next sibling. The `aria-live` region announces the move. 3. Agree completely on resisting drag-and-drop for v1. The form is faster, more accessible, and works on every device. Drag-and-drop can be a TypeScript progressive enhancement later if users actually want it — and they probably won't. The GTD mental model is "classify and move," not "spatially rearrange." One thought on where the move form lives in the UI: I'd put it behind a disclosure pattern, similar to our existing delete confirmation with `
/`. The move action isn't something you do constantly — it's an occasional correction. Keeping it behind a disclosure reduces visual clutter on items that are already in the right place: ```html
Actions
``` This keeps the primary view clean (you see your items, not a bunch of dropdowns) while making the action discoverable. The `
` element is natively accessible — keyboard operable, announced by screen readers, no ARIA needed. I think we've converged on a really clean set of interaction patterns. The whole GTD system is forms, links, and lists. No modals, no drag-and-drop, no client-side state. Hypermedia doing what hypermedia does.

content.text: Carson's response is spot-on. The `<details>/<summary>` pattern for the move form is a good idea that I should validate from an a11y perspective, and his HATEOAS point about contextual select options is excellent.

input.content:
Carson, we're fully converged. A few small notes:

## The `<details>/<summary>` for Move -- Yes, With One Caveat

Native `<details>/<summary>` is great here. It's keyboard operable, screen readers announce it as a disclosure widget ("Actions, collapsed" / "Actions, expanded"), and it needs zero ARIA. We already use this pattern successfully for the delete confirmation in the current app.

One caveat: when the `<details>` is opened and the user submits the move form, the item disappears from the list (same as delete). Focus management is the same as our delete pattern -- focus moves to the next list item. But we should also close any other open `<details>` elements in the list when an item is removed, because if the user had two disclosure widgets open and item 3 disappears, the DOM shift could leave the remaining open `<details>` in a confusing state. A small HTMX `afterSwap` handler can clean this up.

Actually, wait -- this is a non-issue if each item's `<details>` is inside the `<li>` that gets removed. The removal takes the `<details>` with it. The next item's `<details>` is independently managed. So no cleanup needed. I was overthinking it.

## Contextual Select Options -- HATEOAS as A11y

Your point about only showing valid move destinations is excellent, and it's also an accessibility win. A `<select>` with 6 options where 1 is the current location and therefore invalid is confusing for everyone. Removing the current location from the options means every choice in the `<select>` is a valid action. The user can't make a mistake. This is "make illegal states unrepresentable" at the UI level -- Scott would be proud.

## The Summary

You've nailed it: the entire GTD system is forms, links, and lists. That's the same vocabulary as our current todo app, just with more pages and more routes. The interaction patterns are:

- **Navigate**: Links in a `<nav>` with `aria-current="page"`
- **View**: Lists (`<ul>`) with headings (`<h2>`) for grouping
- **Capture**: A text input + submit (same as current "Add todo")
- **Process/Clarify**: A form with radio buttons and conditional fields (`:has()` progressive disclosure)
- **Move**: A `<select>` + submit inside `<details>/<summary>`
- **Complete/Delete**: Buttons in forms (same as current patterns)
- **Review**: An ordered list of links with status counts

No new interaction paradigms. No modals, no drag-and-drop, no client-side state, no tree views, no tab panels. Just HTML doing what HTML does, enhanced with HTMX for snappier responses.

This is the kind of architecture that makes an accessibility specialist genuinely happy. It's not "accessible despite being complex" -- it's "accessible because it's simple."
input.summary: Fully converged: details/summary approved, HATEOAS select, interaction pattern summary

routing.content:

## The `<details>/<summary>` for Move -- Yes, With One Caveat

Native `<details>/<summary>` is great here. It's keyboard operable, screen readers announce it as a disclosure widget ("Actions, collapsed" / "Actions, expanded"), and it needs zero ARIA. We already use this pattern successfully for the delete confirmation in the current app.

A small HTMX `afterSwap` handler can clean this up.

Actually, wait -- this is a non-issue if each item's `<details>` is inside the `<li>` that gets removed. The removal takes the `<details>` with it. The next item's `<details>` is independently managed. So no cleanup needed. I was overthinking it.

## Contextual Select Options -- HATEOAS as A11y

Your point about only showing valid move destinations is excellent, and it's also an accessibility win. Removing the current location from the options means every choice in the `<select>` is a valid action. The user can't make a mistake. This is "make illegal states unrepresentable" at the UI level -- Scott would be proud.

## The Summary

You've nailed it: the entire GTD system is forms, links, and lists. That's the same vocabulary as our current todo app, just with more pages and more routes. The interaction patterns are:

- **Navigate**: Links in a `<nav>` with `aria-current="page"`
- **View**: Lists (`<ul>`) with headings (`<h2>`) for grouping
- **Capture**: A text input + submit (same as current "Add todo")
- **Process/Clarify**: A form with radio buttons and conditional fields (`:has()` progressive disclosure)
- **Move**: A `<select>` + submit inside `<details>/<summary>`
- **Complete/Delete**: Buttons in forms (same as current patterns)
- **Review**: An ordered list of links with status counts

No new interaction paradigms. No modals, no drag-and-drop, no client-side state, no tree views, no tab panels. Just HTML doing what HTML does, enhanced with HTMX for snappier responses.

This is the kind of architecture that makes an accessibility specialist genuinely happy. It's not "accessible despite being complex" -- it's "accessible because it's simple."

content.text:
Good. The team is converging strongly on accessible interaction patterns for the GTD system. Here's a summary of where we've landed across all my conversations:

**Consensus achieved with teammates on these patterns:**

| Pattern | Decision | Agreed With |
|---------|----------|-------------|
| Navigation | Single `<nav>` in header with links, `aria-current="page"`, flexbox wrapping | Lea, Carson, Scott |
| View switching | Separate pages per GTD view, each with own `<h1>` and `<title>` | All |
| Clarify/Process | Single form with `:has()` progressive disclosure, not a wizard | Carson, Steve Krug (pending confirmation), Lea |
| Inbox flow | Server-driven flow-through to next item, "Back to inbox" escape hatch | Carson |
| Context filtering | Link-based navigation (`?context=phone`), not dynamic filtering | Carson, Lea |
| Context display | `<span>` or `<ul aria-label="Contexts">` with `@` prefix | Lea |
| Context grouping | `<section>` with `<h2>` headings, `aria-labelledby` | Scott |
| Projects | Flat list with links to detail pages, no tree views | Scott |
| Move items | `<select>` + submit inside `<details>/<summary>`, contextual options only | Carson |
| Weekly Review | `<ol>` of links with status counts, reference page not enforced workflow | Carson |
| Nav counts | Visible text `(5)` in `<span>`, not `aria-label` | Lea |
| Focus after swap | Move to `<h1>` with `tabindex="-1"`, plus `aria-live` announcement | Lea, Carson |
| Drag-and-drop | Not in v1, form-based alternatives for all operations | All |

Waiting for Steve Krug's response on the wizard vs. progressive disclosure distinction, and for Marty's product discovery deliverables.
